medications.indication_rationale: Topical imidazole to treat candida; a short course combined with low-potency hydrocortisone can settle the acute inflammation/burning.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to affected folds twice daily (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Barrier over treated skin to reduce friction/maceration once antifungal applied.
A potent steroid without antifungal cover can worsen candidal infection; avoid.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Gently pat the folds fully dry after washing (cool hairdryer for deep folds), keep skin surfaces separated, and wear loose clothing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing moisture is essential — candida thrives in damp folds.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Work with your GP on blood-sugar control.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Better glycaemic control reduces recurrence and speeds healing.

patient_education_points.point: The recent antibiotics and high blood sugars let a natural yeast overgrow in the warm, damp skin folds — the antifungal cream treats it and the barrier paste protects the raw skin.
Keeping the folds dry and separated is as important as the cream. Improvement in the burning should come within 24–48 hours; full clearance takes 1–2 weeks.
If the redness spreads and hardens beyond the folds, becomes thick yellow pus, or you develop a fever — seek urgent medical care.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Clinical response, any secondary bacterial infection, and diabetes optimisation.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading/hardening redness, thick pus, or fever — seek urgent care.

must_include_items: Diagnose candidal intertrigo (satellite pustules/pattern) and distinguish from tinea/erythrasma/inverse psoriasis
Topical antifungal (± short low-potency steroid) plus drying/barrier care
Safety-net for secondary bacterial cellulitis (higher risk in diabetes)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires correct candidal-intertrigo management (antifungal + drying/barrier), attention to precipitants, and a diabetic cellulitis safety-net.
